title: ARDS: Lung-Protective Ventilation and Coordinated ICU Care
subtitle: A 56-year-old man with severe pneumonia develops ARDS — bilateral infiltrates and refractory hypoxemia on 100% oxygen. The nurse supports lung-protective ventilation, coordinates a prone-positioning turn, and watches for complications.

patient.name: Victor Hale
patient.admitDate: Medical ICU, intubated for ARDS from severe bacterial pneumonia. Bilateral infiltrates with refractory hypoxemia (SpO2 86% on FiO2 100%) and a low PaO2/FiO2 ratio. On lung-protective ventilation; team coordinating prone positioning.

vitals.temp: Sudden desat, absent breath sounds one side

nursesNote: Victor Hale, 56, intubated for ARDS from severe pneumonia: bilateral infiltrates, refractory hypoxemia (SpO2 86% on 100% FiO2), PaO2/FiO2 ~90. Managed with lung-protective ventilation — low tidal volume (~6 mL/kg IBW), adequate PEEP, plateau <30, FiO2 titrated to the lowest level for SpO2 88-95% with permissive hypercapnia. Coordinating a safe prone-positioning turn with the team. Treating the pneumonia, providing sedation/comfort, and monitoring for barotrauma/pneumothorax while keeping the interdisciplinary team and family updated.
providerOrders: Lung-protective ventilation: low tidal volume (~6 mL/kg IBW), adequate PEEP, plateau pressure <30
Titrate FiO2 to the lowest level for SpO2 88-95%; permissive hypercapnia acceptable
Prone positioning for severe refractory hypoxemia; coordinate the turn team
Sedation/analgesia; conservative fluids; treat the underlying pneumonia
VAP and DVT/PUD prophylaxis; interdisciplinary daily goals; keep family updated

opts.text: ARDS is acute, with bilateral infiltrates and severe hypoxemia not caused by heart failure
The hypoxemia is refractory — it stays low despite high supplemental oxygen
A low PaO2/FiO2 ratio reflects the severity
It often follows sepsis, pneumonia, aspiration, or major trauma
ARDS hypoxemia improves quickly with a simple nasal cannula
These patients usually need intubation and mechanical ventilation
Lung-protective ventilation uses LOW tidal volumes to limit injury
Adequate PEEP recruits alveoli and improves oxygenation
Permissive hypercapnia may be accepted to protect the lungs
Prone positioning can improve oxygenation in severe ARDS
High tidal volumes are preferred to fully expand the lungs
Care is coordinated with respiratory therapy and the ICU team
Confirm severe refractory hypoxemia and secure airway/ventilation
Apply lung-protective settings (low tidal volume, PEEP, plateau <30)
Titrate FiO2 to the lowest level meeting the oxygenation goal
Coordinate prone positioning for refractory hypoxemia
Treat the underlying cause and reassess; document and communicate

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.sub: Lung-protective or harmful to ARDS lungs?

opts.text: Low tidal volume ~6 mL/kg ideal body weight
Adequate PEEP to keep alveoli open
Keep plateau pressure below 30 cm H2O
Titrate FiO2 to the lowest level meeting the SpO2 goal
Prone positioning for severe refractory hypoxemia
Large tidal volumes ~12 mL/kg to "blow off" CO2
High plateau pressures with no PEEP
Keeping FiO2 at 100% indefinitely once oxygenation is adequate

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Verify ventilator settings and oxygenation with respiratory therapy
Ensure lung-protective parameters and a sedation/comfort plan
Organize and lead a safe prone turn, protecting the ETT and lines
Provide skin/eye/pressure care and reposition per protocol while prone
Update the interdisciplinary team and family; document the response
Oxygenation improves and the PaO2/FiO2 ratio rises after proning
FiO2 is weaned down while SpO2 stays at goal
Lung-protective settings are maintained with plateau pressure <30
The infection is treated and the patient meets readiness for a breathing trial
Sudden desaturation with absent breath sounds on one side
Oxygenation drops further and the patient becomes hypotensive
An ETT or central line is dislodged during an uncoordinated turn
High tidal volumes are used and the plateau pressure climbs above 30

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Physiological Adaptation, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
